examples.japanese_sentence: {私|わたし}はエンジニアです。
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle は (wa) marks the topic of the sentence, in this case, the speaker.

examples.japanese_sentence: {私|わたし}の{本|ほん}を{返|かえ}してください。
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle の (no) is used to indicate possession.

examples.japanese_sentence: {私|わたし}たちは{昨日|きのう}{日本|にほん}に{来|き}ました。
examples.grammar_explanation: The suffix ～たち (~tachi) is added to a pronoun to make it plural.

examples.japanese_sentence: {私|わたし}たちも{行|い}くよ。
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle も (mo) means 'also' or 'too' and replaces the topic marker は.

examples.japanese_sentence: あなたは{学生|がくせい}ですか。
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle か (ka) at the end of a sentence indicates a question in formal Japanese.

examples.japanese_sentence: あなたはどこに{住|す}んでいるの？
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle の (no) at the end of a casual sentence acts as a soft question marker.

examples.japanese_sentence: {あの人|あのひと}は{誰|だれ}ですか。
examples.grammar_explanation: あの (ano) is a demonstrative adjective that refers to someone far from both the speaker and listener.

examples.japanese_sentence: {あの方|あのかた}はどなたですか。
examples.grammar_explanation: 方 (kata) is the polite equivalent of 人 (hito).

examples.japanese_sentence: {皆さん|みなさん}、おはようございます。
examples.grammar_explanation: Adding ～さん (~san) to 皆 (mina) makes it more polite and respectful when addressing a group.

examples.japanese_sentence: {皆さん|みなさん}にこれをあげます。
examples.english_translation: I will give this to everyone.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle に (ni) marks the recipient of the action.

examples.japanese_sentence: {田中|たなか}さんは{会社員|かいしゃいん}です。
examples.english_translation: Mr. Tanaka is a company employee.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～さん (~san) is the standard honorific suffix used for adults.

examples.japanese_sentence: マリアさんはアメリカから{来|き}ました。
examples.grammar_explanation: ～からきました (~kara kimashita) is used to state where someone originates from.

vocabulary.meaning_english: suffix added to a child’s name

examples.japanese_sentence: {太郎|たろう}ちゃんは5{歳|さい}です。
examples.grammar_explanation: ～ちゃん (~chan) is an affectionate suffix used mainly for children or pets.

examples.japanese_sentence: {花子|はなこ}ちゃん、あそぼう！
examples.grammar_explanation: The volitional form ～おう (~ou) is used here for a casual invitation to do something.

vocabulary.meaning_english: suffix added to a boy’s name

examples.japanese_sentence: {佐藤|さとう}くん、その{辞書|じしょ}を{貸|か}して。
examples.grammar_explanation: ～くん (~kun) is typically used for boys or by superiors when addressing male subordinates.

examples.japanese_sentence: {山田|やまだ}くんはどこ？
examples.grammar_explanation: In casual speech, the copula 'desu' is often omitted in simple questions.

examples.japanese_sentence: {私|わたし}は{日本|にほん}{人|じん}です。
examples.grammar_explanation: The suffix ～人 (jin) is attached to a country name to indicate nationality.

examples.japanese_sentence: あの{人|ひと}はアメリカ{人|じん}じゃないです。
examples.grammar_explanation: ～じゃないです (~janai desu) is the negative form of the copula 'desu'.

examples.japanese_sentence: {先生|せんせい}に{質問|しつもん}しました。
examples.english_translation: I asked the teacher a question.
examples.grammar_explanation: 先生 (sensei) is used as a title or a way to address a teacher directly.

examples.japanese_sentence: {田中|たなか}{先生|せんせい}はとても{優|やさ}しい。
examples.grammar_explanation: The adjective 優 (yasa) shii ends in 'i', making it a standard i-adjective used in the dictionary form.

examples.japanese_sentence: {私|わたし}の{職業|しょくぎょう}は{教師|きょうし}です。
examples.grammar_explanation: 教師 (kyoushi) is the term used to describe teaching as a profession, rather than as an honorific title.

examples.japanese_sentence: {母|はは}は{大学|だいがく}の{教師|きょうし}をしています。
examples.english_translation: My mother works as a university teacher.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～をしています (~o shite imasu) is used to describe a current occupation or role.

examples.japanese_sentence: {私|わたし}は{大学|だいがく}の{学生|がくせい}です。
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle の (no) connects two nouns, specifying what kind of student.

examples.japanese_sentence: かれは{学生|がくせい}じゃないよ。
examples.grammar_explanation: The sentence-final particle よ (yo) adds emphasis to the statement.

examples.japanese_sentence: {田中|たなか}さんはIMCの{会社員|かいしゃいん}です。
examples.english_translation: Mr. Tanaka is an employee of IMC.
examples.grammar_explanation: The noun 会社員 (kaishain) refers to a person's general status as a corporate worker.

examples.japanese_sentence: {会社員|かいしゃいん}になりたいです。
examples.english_translation: I want to become a company employee.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～になりたい (~ni naritai) expresses the desire to become something.

examples.japanese_sentence: かれはIMCの{社員|しゃいん}です。
examples.english_translation: He is an employee of IMC.
examples.grammar_explanation: 社員 (shain) is used when mentioning a person as an employee of a specific named company.

examples.japanese_sentence: {社員|しゃいん}を{集|あつ}めてください。
examples.grammar_explanation: The verb 集 (atsu) meru is in the te-form plus 'kudasai' for a request.

examples.japanese_sentence: {姉|あね}は{銀行員|ぎんこういん}です。
examples.english_translation: My older sister is a bank employee.
examples.grammar_explanation: 銀行 (ginkou) means bank, and the suffix ～員 (in) means member/staff.

examples.japanese_sentence: {銀行員|ぎんこういん}になりたかった。
examples.english_translation: I wanted to be a bank employee.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～たかった (~takatta) is the past tense of the desiderative form (~tai).

examples.japanese_sentence: {病院|びょういん}で{医者|いしゃ}に{会|あ}いました。
examples.english_translation: I met a doctor at the hospital.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle に (ni) marks the person you meet (会う).

examples.japanese_sentence: {将来|しょうらい}、{医者|いしゃ}になるつもりです。
examples.english_translation: I intend to become a doctor in the future.
examples.grammar_explanation: つもり (tsumori) indicates a plan or intention.

examples.japanese_sentence: あの方は{大学|だいがく}の{研究者|けんきゅうしゃ}です。
examples.english_translation: That person is a university researcher.
examples.grammar_explanation: 研究 (kenkyuu) means research, and 者 (sha) means person/professional.

examples.japanese_sentence: {研究者|けんきゅうしゃ}は{忙|いそが}しいですか。
examples.grammar_explanation: The adjective 忙 (isoga) shii is used as a predicate.

examples.japanese_sentence: サントスさんはエンジニアではありません。
examples.english_translation: Mr. Santos is not an engineer.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～ではありません (~dewa arimasen) is the formal negative form of the copula 'desu'.

examples.japanese_sentence: エンジニアを{探|さが}しています。
examples.english_translation: I am looking for an engineer.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～ています (~te imasu) indicates a continuous action in progress.

examples.japanese_sentence: この{大学|だいがく}は{有名|ゆうめい}です。
examples.grammar_explanation: 有名 (yuumei) is a na-adjective used here as a predicate.

examples.japanese_sentence: {大学|だいがく}で{勉強|べんきょう}します。
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle で (de) marks the location where an action takes place.

examples.japanese_sentence: {昨日|きのう}{病院|びょういん}へ{行|い}きました。
examples.english_translation: I went to the hospital yesterday.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle へ (e) indicates direction or destination.

examples.japanese_sentence: {病院|びょういん}はどこ？
examples.grammar_explanation: どこ (doko) is the interrogative pronoun for location.

examples.japanese_sentence: {電気|でんき}を{消|け}してください。
examples.grammar_explanation: 電気 (denki) can mean both electricity and the physical light fixture.

examples.japanese_sentence: {電気|でんき}がついていない。
examples.grammar_explanation: ～ていない (~te inai) is the negative continuous/state form.

examples.japanese_sentence: {誰|だれ}が{来|き}ましたか。
examples.grammar_explanation: When the subject of a sentence is an interrogative like 誰, the particle が (ga) must be used instead of は.

examples.japanese_sentence: どなたですか。
examples.grammar_explanation: どなた (donata) is the honorific version of 誰.

examples.japanese_sentence: {私|わたし}は25{歳|さい}です。
examples.grammar_explanation: The counter ～歳 (sai) is used for age.

examples.japanese_sentence: ワンさんは8{歳|さい}でした。
examples.grammar_explanation: ～でした (~deshita) is the past tense of the copula 'desu'.

examples.japanese_sentence: お子さんは{何歳|なんさい}ですか。
examples.grammar_explanation: 何歳 (nansai) is the standard question for age.

examples.japanese_sentence: おいくつですか。
examples.grammar_explanation: おいくつ (oikutsu) is the honorific expression for asking someone's age.

examples.japanese_sentence: はい、そうです。
examples.grammar_explanation: はい (hai) is a formal affirmation used in response to questions.

examples.japanese_sentence: はい、わかりました。
examples.grammar_explanation: The verb わかる is used in its past form to show comprehension has occurred.

examples.japanese_sentence: いいえ、{違|ちが}います。
examples.grammar_explanation: いいえ (iie) is the formal way to say no/negate a statement.

examples.japanese_sentence: いいえ、まだです。
examples.grammar_explanation: まだ (mada) indicates that something has not happened yet.

examples.japanese_sentence: {失礼|しつれい}ですが、お{名前|なまえ}は？
examples.english_translation: Excuse me, but what is your name?
examples.grammar_explanation: ～ですが (~desu ga) is used to soften the introduction of a personal question.

examples.japanese_sentence: {失礼|しつれい}ですが、どなたですか。
examples.english_translation: Excuse me, but who are you?
examples.grammar_explanation: This phrase is essential for politeness when asking for identity.

examples.japanese_sentence: お{名前|なまえ}は？
examples.grammar_explanation: The honorific 'o' is added to 'namae' for politeness; the end of the sentence is implied.

examples.japanese_sentence: お{名前|なまえ}を{書|か}いてください。
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle を (o) marks 'name' as the direct object of 'write'.

vocabulary.meaning_english: how do you do? introducing oneself for the first time

examples.japanese_sentence: {初|はじ}めまして、ジョンです。
examples.english_translation: How do you do? I am John.
examples.grammar_explanation: This phrase comes from the verb 始める (to begin) and is only used the first time people meet.

examples.japanese_sentence: {初|はじ}めまして、よろしく。
examples.english_translation: Nice to meet you, please be kind to me.
examples.grammar_explanation: よろしく (yoroshiku) is a casual shortening of the full formal greeting.

vocabulary.word_kanji: どうぞよろしくおねがいします

examples.japanese_sentence: {初|はじ}めまして、{私|わたし}はマリアです。どうぞよろしくおねがいします。
examples.english_translation: How do you do? I am Maria. Pleased to meet you.
examples.grammar_explanation: This is the most standard formal introduction structure.

examples.japanese_sentence: これからお{世話|せわ}になります。どうぞよろしくおねがいします。
examples.english_translation: I will be in your care from now on. Please treat me well.
examples.grammar_explanation: This set phrase often follows the introduction when starting a new job or school.

examples.japanese_sentence: こちらはミラーさんです。
examples.grammar_explanation: こちら (kochira) is a polite demonstrative meaning 'this way' or 'this person'.

examples.japanese_sentence: こちらは{父|ちち}です。
examples.grammar_explanation: When introducing family, honorifics like ～さん are not used.

examples.japanese_sentence: {私|わたし}はインドネシアから{来|き}ました。
examples.grammar_explanation: から (kara) means 'from' and refers to the origin of travel or birth.

examples.japanese_sentence: どこから{来|き}たの？
examples.grammar_explanation: A casual past tense question using the short form of the verb.

examples.japanese_sentence: アメリカはとても{広|ひろ}いです。
examples.grammar_explanation: The i-adjective 広 (hiro) i is used to describe geography.

examples.japanese_sentence: いつアメリカへ{行|い}きますか。
examples.english_translation: When are you going to America?
examples.grammar_explanation: いつ (itsu) is the interrogative for 'when'.

examples.japanese_sentence: イギリスから{来|き}た{学生|がくせい}です。
examples.english_translation: I am a student who came from the UK.
examples.grammar_explanation: The verb phrase 'came from the UK' modifies the noun 'student'.

examples.japanese_sentence: イギリスの{天気|てんき}はどうですか。
examples.english_translation: How is the weather in the UK?
examples.grammar_explanation: どうですか (dou desu ka) is used to ask for an opinion or description.

examples.japanese_sentence: インドのカレーは{辛|から}いですか。
examples.grammar_explanation: The noun インド modifies カレー using the particle の.

examples.japanese_sentence: インドへ{行|い}ったことがありますか。
examples.english_translation: Have you ever been to India?
examples.grammar_explanation: ～たことがあります (~ta koto ga arimasu) is used to describe past experience.

examples.japanese_sentence: インドネシアの{首都|しゅと}はジャカルタです。
examples.english_translation: The capital of Indonesia is Jakarta.
examples.grammar_explanation: This follows the AはBです sentence pattern.

examples.japanese_sentence: インドネシアへ{旅行|りょこう}しました。
examples.grammar_explanation: 旅行する (ryokou suru) is a compound verb used in the past tense.

examples.japanese_sentence: {韓国|かんこく}の{料理|りょうり}を{食|た}べたいです。
examples.english_translation: I want to eat Korean food.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～たい (~tai) is added to the verb stem to express the speaker's desire.

examples.japanese_sentence: {韓国|かんこく}ドラマが{好|す}きです。
examples.grammar_explanation: 好き (suki) is a na-adjective that takes the particle が to mark the object of liking.

examples.japanese_sentence: タイは{一年中|いちねんじゅう}{暑|あつ}いです。
examples.english_translation: It is hot all year round in Thailand.
examples.grammar_explanation: The i-adjective 暑 (atsu) i describes temperature/climate.

examples.japanese_sentence: タイへ{行|い}こう！
examples.grammar_explanation: 行こう (ikou) is the casual volitional form of the verb 行く.

examples.japanese_sentence: ドイツのビールは{有名|ゆうめい}です。
examples.grammar_explanation: ドイツ modifies ビール to specify the type/origin.

examples.japanese_sentence: ドイツ{語|ご}ができますか。
examples.grammar_explanation: The suffix ～語 (go) indicates a language, and できます (dekimasu) indicates ability.

examples.japanese_sentence: フランスのパンを{買|か}いました。
examples.grammar_explanation: The past tense verb 買いました (kaimashita) ends the sentence.

examples.japanese_sentence: フランスへ{行|い}った。
examples.grammar_explanation: The plain past form of the verb is used in casual conversation.

examples.japanese_sentence: ブラジルはコーヒーが{有名|ゆうめい}です。
examples.english_translation: Brazil is famous for its coffee.
examples.grammar_explanation: The topic is Brazil, but the specific attribute is marked by が.

examples.japanese_sentence: ブラジルから{友達|ともだち}が{来|き}ます。
examples.english_translation: A friend is coming from Brazil.
examples.grammar_explanation: 来ます (kimasu) indicates movement toward the speaker's location.

examples.japanese_sentence: {日本|にほん}の{冬|ふゆ}は{寒|さむ}いです。
examples.grammar_explanation: The i-adjective 寒 (samu) i is used to describe the cold weather.

examples.japanese_sentence: {日本|にほん}に{住|す}みたい。
examples.english_translation: I want to live in Japan.
examples.grammar_explanation: 住みたい (sumitai) is the casual form of the desire to live somewhere.

examples.japanese_sentence: フィリピンのマンゴーは{甘|あま}いです。
examples.grammar_explanation: 甘 (ama) i is an i-adjective used as a predicate.

examples.japanese_sentence: フィリピンへ{行|い}きませんか。
examples.english_translation: Won't you go to the Philippines (with me)?
examples.grammar_explanation: ～ませんか (~masen ka) is used to make a polite invitation.
